title: Scratches Are Too Deep
description: The surface has visible sanding marks, gouges, or scratches that remain after sanding.

solutions.title: Deep scratches remain after coarse grit
solutions.likelyCause: The starting grit was too coarse for the finish goal, or the next grit was not fine enough to remove the previous scratch pattern.
solutions.recommendedGrit: Move one step finer at a time: 80 → 120 → 180 → 220 → 320. For finishing, continue to 400+ as needed.
solutions.method: Dry for early removal; wet for fine finishing when the surface and project allow it.
steps: Stop sanding aggressively in one spot.
Sand evenly with the next finer grit until the old scratch pattern is lighter.
Clean the surface before moving to the next grit.
Repeat with finer grits until the scratches are no longer visible.
solutions.avoid: Do not jump directly from a very coarse grit to a very fine grit.
solutions.successCheck: The surface shows one even, finer scratch pattern with no remaining deep lines.

solutions.title: 320 grit does not remove scratches from 180 grit
solutions.likelyCause: The grit jump is too large or the 180 grit scratches were too deep from excess pressure.
solutions.recommendedGrit: Use 220 or 240 grit first, then 320 grit.
solutions.method: Dry for most prep work; wet only for fine finish stages where appropriate.
steps: Return to 220 or 240 grit.
Sand with lighter pressure and overlapping strokes.
Clean the surface.
Move to 320 grit only after the 180-grit scratches are gone.
solutions.avoid: Do not press harder with 320 grit to force it to remove deep scratches.
solutions.successCheck: The surface has no visible 180-grit lines before switching back to 320.

title: Not Sure What Grit To Use
description: The customer does not know which grit to start with or which grit comes next.

solutions.title: Choose the right starting grit
solutions.likelyCause: The task stage is not defined yet: removal, prep, finish, or polishing prep.
solutions.recommendedGrit: 60–120 for heavy removal; 150–240 for prep; 280–400 for finish prep; 600–800 for extra-fine finishing; 1000–3000 for polishing preparation.
solutions.method: Dry for removal and prep; wet for fine finishing and polishing preparation.
steps: Identify the task: remove, smooth, finish, or polish prep.
Start with the least aggressive grit that can still do the job.
Move finer gradually.
Check the surface before each grit change.
solutions.avoid: Do not start with 60 or 80 grit on a surface that only needs light smoothing.
solutions.successCheck: The selected grit removes the defect without creating unnecessary deep scratches.

title: Sandpaper Clogs Too Fast
description: Dust, paint, finish, or residue loads into the abrasive surface quickly.

solutions.title: Paper clogs while sanding paint or finish
solutions.likelyCause: Soft paint, finish residue, or dust buildup is loading the abrasive surface.
solutions.recommendedGrit: Use the correct prep grit for the stage; move to wet sanding on finer grits when appropriate.
solutions.method: Dry for removal; wet for fine finish stages to reduce loading.
steps: Clean the surface before sanding.
Use lighter pressure.
Wipe dust or residue often.
Replace the sheet when cutting slows.
Use wet sanding for finer finishing where suitable.
solutions.avoid: Do not keep sanding with a fully loaded sheet.
solutions.successCheck: The sheet continues cutting and the surface does not smear or gum up.

title: Sanding Takes Too Long
description: The sandpaper is cutting slowly or the task feels inefficient.

solutions.title: The starting grit is too fine
solutions.likelyCause: A fine grit is being used for a removal job that needs a coarser grit first.
solutions.recommendedGrit: Use 60–120 for heavy removal, then step up to finer grits.
solutions.method: Dry sanding is usually faster for early removal.
steps: Switch to a coarser grit suited to the removal stage.
Sand evenly until the defect is reduced.
Move to the next finer grit to refine the surface.
Replace worn sheets promptly.
solutions.avoid: Do not use 400+ grit for heavy removal.
solutions.successCheck: Material removal becomes faster without needing excessive pressure.

title: Finish Looks Uneven
description: The surface has uneven dull spots, patchy sanding marks, or inconsistent texture.

solutions.title: Uneven pressure caused patchy sanding
solutions.likelyCause: Pressure, stroke direction, or time spent in each area was inconsistent.
solutions.recommendedGrit: Use the same grit already selected for the stage, then move finer only after the surface is even.
solutions.method: Dry or wet depending on the stage; keep pressure light and consistent.
steps: Sand with overlapping strokes.
Keep pressure even.
Use a sanding block on flat surfaces.
Clean and inspect the surface often.
Move to the next grit only after the surface is uniform.
solutions.avoid: Do not focus pressure on one small area for too long.
solutions.successCheck: The surface has a consistent scratch pattern and uniform appearance.

title: Swirl Marks Remain
description: Circular or curved marks remain visible after sanding or wet sanding.

solutions.title: Final grit is too coarse for polishing prep
solutions.likelyCause: The final grit did not refine the surface enough, or the sanding pattern was inconsistent.
solutions.recommendedGrit: Continue with finer grits such as 1000, 1500, 2000, and 3000 as needed for polishing preparation.
solutions.method: Wet sanding for fine finishing and polishing preparation.
steps: Clean the surface.
Use light pressure with fine grit.
Sand evenly with controlled strokes.
Move to the next finer grit only after the previous marks are uniform.
Prepare for polishing after the final fine grit.
solutions.avoid: Do not press harder to remove swirl marks with a coarse grit.
solutions.successCheck: The surface has a uniform fine haze with no deeper circular marks.

title: Paper Tears Early
description: The sheet rips, catches, or wears through before expected.

solutions.title: Paper tears on sharp edges or corners
solutions.likelyCause: The sheet is catching on sharp corners, burrs, or rough raised edges.
solutions.recommendedGrit: Use the correct grit for the surface, but reduce pressure around edges.
solutions.method: Dry or wet depending on task; use lighter pressure near edges.
steps: Inspect the edge before sanding.
Reduce pressure near corners.
Fold the sheet carefully or use a sanding block where suitable.
Avoid pulling the sheet hard across sharp edges.
solutions.avoid: Do not force the sheet into a sharp corner under heavy pressure.
solutions.successCheck: The sheet moves smoothly without catching or tearing.

title: Poor Results Between Coats
description: Sanding between paint, primer, sealer, or clear coats leaves visible problems.

solutions.title: The grit is too coarse between coats
solutions.likelyCause: A coarse grit removed too much coating or left scratches that show through the next layer.
solutions.recommendedGrit: Use fine grits such as 320–600 depending on coating stage and finish goal.
solutions.method: Light dry sanding or wet sanding for finer finish stages where appropriate.
steps: Let the coating cure according to the coating instructions.
Use light pressure.
Sand only enough to smooth and prepare the surface.
Clean the surface fully before the next coat.
solutions.avoid: Do not use heavy-removal grits between finish coats.
solutions.successCheck: The surface feels smooth and lightly prepared without cutting through the coating.

title: Wet Sanding Leaves Haze
description: Wet sanding leaves a cloudy, dull, or hazy surface.
aliases: dull clear coat after wet sanding

solutions.title: Haze remains after fine wet sanding
solutions.likelyCause: Fine wet sanding normally leaves a uniform dull haze that must be refined with finer grits and then polished where the project requires gloss.
solutions.recommendedGrit: Progress through finer grits, commonly 1000 → 1500 → 2000 → 3000 for polishing preparation.
solutions.method: Wet sanding with clean water and light pressure.
steps: Keep the surface and paper wet.
Use light pressure.
Refine with the next finer grit.
Wipe and inspect between grits.
Polish after final sanding where a gloss finish is required.
solutions.avoid: Do not expect high gloss immediately after wet sanding alone.
solutions.successCheck: The haze is even, fine, and ready for polishing or the next finish step.

title: Surface Still Feels Rough
description: The surface does not become smooth even after sanding.

solutions.title: The surface needs gradual grit progression
solutions.likelyCause: The surface was not fully leveled at the current grit before moving finer, or grit steps were skipped.
solutions.recommendedGrit: Use the current working grit until the surface is consistent, then step up gradually.
solutions.method: Dry for prep; wet for fine finishing when appropriate.
steps: Return to the grit that still cuts the roughness.
Sand until the surface feels consistent.
Clean the surface.
Move to the next finer grit.
Repeat until the desired smoothness is reached.
solutions.avoid: Do not move to very fine grit before the roughness is removed.
solutions.successCheck: The surface feels even before moving to the next grit.
